pulls.title: 8388018: [IR Framework] Be more lenient about system properties
pulls.author: marc-chevalier
pulls.body_excerpt: When I run a sub-test in the IR framework, I often run a single scenario, since it is common it's some specific set of flags that causes the issue. But it is not rare, if not common, that I run more than one test. Yet, the system properties are `-DScenarios` (plural) and `-DTest` (singular). That doesn't reflect the common cases. Moreover, it is not quite rare to mix up the case by accident, and then nothing tells the problem. pulls.title: 8361099: Shenandoah: Implement CAS-based allocator
pulls.author: pengxiaolong
pulls.body_excerpt: This introduces a lock-free CAS-based allocation path for Shenandoah's mutator/collector/old-collector partitions, replacing (or supplementing) the heap-locked serial allocator on the fast path. Each ShenandoahPartitionAllocator maintains a small stripe of cached "alloc regions" per partition; threads map to a stripe slot and bump-allocate via CAS on the region's _atomic_top without the heap lock. When a slot is exhausted, a heap-locked slow path replenishes it from the free set. pulls.title: 8388561: [s390x] Prevent accidental page table expansion
pulls.author: tstuefe
pulls.body_excerpt: Linux s390x is special in that the kernel can expand a process' page table dynamically *during its runtime*. That is pretty cool, but it poses some problems for us. Expansion happens in three steps: - three levels (42 bits, max 4TB) - 4 levels (53 bits, 8PB) - 5 levels (64 bits, 16EB). It only works one-way - once switched to a higher level, there is no way back. Expansion is triggered when an allocation attempt cannot be satisfied within the confines of the current user address space. Either because its too large, and/or the address space is too fragmented. pulls.title: 8387045: JDK with transformer agent hangs on shutdown
pulls.author: dholmes-ora
pulls.body_excerpt: Before posting the VM_DEATH event the JVMTI code spins for up to 60 seconds to allow in-progress callbacks to complete. If one of those callbacks requires a safepoint while executing, then it will not be able to attain it until the 60s spin is over - thus causing an apparent "hang" at VM termination. The simple fix is to insert a `ThreadBlockInVM` in the loop so that safepoint requests are honored. pulls.title: 8384138: Linux container CPU hierarchy adjustment can ignore stricter leaf CPU quotas
pulls.author: caspernorrbin
pulls.body_excerpt: Hi everyone, When traversing the cgroup hierarchy while adjusting the controller for CPU limits in `CgroupUtil::adjust_controller`, we do not fold the initial (leaf) quota as `lowest_limit`. As a result, a stricter leaf quota can be skipped in favor of a less restrictive quota from a parent cgroup. This change fixes that by updating `lowest_limit` with the leaf value before walking the parent hiererchy, similar to what is already done for the memory controller hierarchy adjustment. pulls.title: 8383246: Add relaxed implementations of atomics for Windows/AArch64
pulls.author: raneashay
pulls.body_excerpt: This patch implements two key changes. First, prior to this patch, the Windows/AArch64 implementation of `PlatformAdd`, `PlatformXchg`, and `PlatformCmpXchg` structs ignored the `atomic_memory_order` parameter and instead used the strongest barrier semantics for all operations. This is correct but unnecessarily conservative and also inconsistent with the Linux and macOS AArch64 ports, both of which dispatch to different instruction sequences based on the requested ordering. pulls.title: 8308105: Multi-array allocations are very slow
pulls.author: bulasevich
pulls.body_excerpt: Multidimensional array allocations such as new int[n1][n2] are very slow because HotSpot routes them through a runtime call (multianewarray) that allocates the outer array and each subarray sequentially. For constant-size cases like new int[2][2], C2 already optimizes during the parse phase by inlining a fixed number of AllocateArray nodes. This change extends that optimization to the variable-size 2D case. pulls.title: 8349452: Fix performance regression for Arrays.fill() with AVX512
pulls.author: vamsi-parasa
pulls.body_excerpt: The goal of this PR is to fix the performance regression in Arrays.fill() x86 stubs caused by masked AVX stores. The fix is to replace the masked AVX stores with store instructions without masks (i.e. unmasked stores). `fill32_masked()` and `fill64_masked()` stubs are replaced with `fill32_unmasked()` and `fill64_unmasked()` respectively. To speedup unmasked stores, array fills for sizes < 64 bytes are broken down into sequences of 32B, 16B, 8B, 4B, 2B and 1B stores, depending on the size. ### **Performance comparison for byte array fills in a loop for 1 million times** UseAVX=3 ByteArray Size

pulls.title: 8359359: AArch64: share trampolines between static calls to the same method
pulls.author: mikabl-arm
pulls.body_excerpt: Modify the C2 compiler to share trampoline stubs between static calls that resolve to the same callee method. Since the relocation target for all static calls is initially set to the static call resolver stub, the call's target alone cannot be used to distinguish between different static method calls. Instead, trampoline stubs should be shared based on the actual callee. The `SharedTrampolineTest.java` was designed to verify the sharing of trampolines among static calls. pulls.title: 8348828: Windows dll loading now resolves symlinks
pulls.author: benjaminp
pulls.body_excerpt: Deep in the bowels of `System.loadLibrary`, `File.getCanonicalPath()` is called on the target library file before it is passed to the system library loading APIs. In JDK-8003887, `File.getCanonicalPath` was altered to resolve symlinks on Windows. This had unintended consequences for passing a symlink to `System.loadLibrary` on Windows. The underlying Windows `LoadLibrary` API inspects the file name passed to it and adds a `.dll` extension if the it is not already present.
